category_summary:
Fundamental debates about AI capabilities and architecture dominated the discourse. **François Chollet** [argued current AI remains bottlenecked](/?date=2026-03-13&category=social#item-8a01a8348a15) by pattern memorization rather than autonomous learning, while **Yann LeCun** and **Judea Pearl** [clashed over causality](/?date=2026-03-13&category=social#item-59f6d60cb195) in world models. **Levelsio's** viral [declaration that **MCP is dead**](/?date=2026-03-13&category=social#item-6ac95f412172) (1.6M views) ignited fierce debate over whether AI needs protocol abstractions at all.

- **Google** [unveiled its biggest **Maps** upgrade](/?date=2026-03-13&category=social#item-c1af3cd981ce) ever with **Gemini** integration, and **Jeff Dean** [shared a **Nature Cancer** study](/?date=2026-03-13&category=social#item-bc7883be35de) showing AI catches 25% of previously missed cancers in NHS screening
- **Perplexity** [launched **Computer**](/?date=2026-03-13&category=social#item-7a7bc12fb52e), a multi-model agent platform with 20+ models and 400+ app connectors for Pro subscribers
- **Neel Nanda** from **Anthropic** [reported that red-teaming](/?date=2026-03-13&category=social#item-ad708e0914e2) Claude's 30K-word constitution showed surprisingly robust alignment improvements
- **Jeremy Howard** [shared contrarian **Answer.AI** research](/?date=2026-03-13&category=social#item-ff7c2ef75465) finding no clear jump in developer productivity from AI coding tools, challenging prevailing hype

**Sam Altman** [framed intelligence as a metered utility](/?date=2026-03-13&category=social#item-a321ab87ca71) at the **BlackRock** summit, while **Matt Shumer** offered pointed feedback that **GPT-5.4** would be perfect if not for its persistent UI generation failures—a sentiment that resonated widely among builders.
category_summary_html:
Fundamental debates about AI capabilities and architecture dominated the discourse. François Chollet argued current AI remains bottlenecked by pattern memorization rather than autonomous learning, while Yann LeCun and Judea Pearl clashed over causality in world models. Levelsio's viral declaration that MCP is dead (1.6M views) ignited fierce debate over whether AI needs protocol abstractions at all.

  • Google unveiled its biggest Maps upgrade ever with Gemini integration, and Jeff Dean shared a Nature Cancer study showing AI catches 25% of previously missed cancers in NHS screening
  • Perplexity launched Computer, a multi-model agent platform with 20+ models and 400+ app connectors for Pro subscribers
  • Neel Nanda from Anthropic reported that red-teaming Claude's 30K-word constitution showed surprisingly robust alignment improvements
  • Jeremy Howard shared contrarian Answer.AI research finding no clear jump in developer productivity from AI coding tools, challenging prevailing hype

Sam Altman framed intelligence as a metered utility at the BlackRock summit, while Matt Shumer offered pointed feedback that GPT-5.4 would be perfect if not for its persistent UI generation failures—a sentiment that resonated widely among builders.

items.title: The bottleneck of current AI is simple: the techniques we use are still predicated on pattern memori...
items.content:
The bottleneck of current AI is simple: the techniques we use are still predicated on pattern memorization and retrieval, and thus they need *someone* to tell them which patterns to memorize (training data, RL envs...)

That role cannot yet be played by AI in a truly open-ended and autonomous way. We can't yet remove the humans in the loop. In that sense, current AI is still purely a reflection of human cognition (both in terms of which tasks/goals it pursues and the patterns it uses to solve them). It isn't yet its own thing.

items.summary: François Chollet argues current AI's bottleneck is pattern memorization and retrieval: AI can't yet autonomously decide which patterns to learn in an open-ended way, making it still a reflection of human cognition rather than its own autonomous intelligence.
items.reasoning: Deeply insightful technical observation from the ARC benchmark creator about fundamental AI limitations. Extremely high engagement (1,167 likes, 125 RTs). Original and thought-provoking analysis of where AI truly stands.

items.title: Excited to see this joint collaboration between @GoogleResearch, @NHSuk and @imperialcollege showing...
items.content:
Excited to see this joint collaboration between @GoogleResearch, @NHSuk and @imperialcollege showing AI’s potential to detect 25% of the interval cancers previously missed by conventional methods. Additionally, the research found AI can reduce screening workloads, and give results back to clinicians and patients faster.

This first figure from the @NatureCancer article shows how the study was set up, and the second figure shows that the AI system dramatically increases sensitivity (detecting true positives) without significantly affecting specificity (false positives).

Learn more ⬇️

Blog: https://t.co/FMhVIVE5nQ

Nature Cancer paper: https://t.co/jjZPbDZxrX

items.title:
@yudapearl Our world models are action conditioned, and hence causal. 

The concept of world model f...
items.content:
@yudapearl Our world models are action conditioned, and hence causal. 

The concept of world model for planning goes back to the 1950s in optimal control (before I was born).
I didn't just discover it. 
But training action-conditioned world models from sensory inputs (like video) requires new techniques.
items.author: @ylecun
items.summary: Continuing our coverage from [yesterday](/?date=2026-03-12&category=social#item-ed24c969f839), Yann LeCun responds to Judea Pearl about world models, explaining that their models are action-conditioned and thus causal, while noting that training such models from sensory inputs like video requires new techniques.
items.reasoning: High-profile exchange between two AI luminaries (LeCun and Pearl) on causality in world models. Technically substantive but a brief reply, not a major announcement. Good engagement.

continuation.original_title: @ronbrachman The basic idea of world models is very old.

items.title:
Sam Altman, at the Blackrock U.S Infrastructure Summit:  

"Fundamentally, our business, and I think...
items.content:
Sam Altman, at the Blackrock U.S Infrastructure Summit:  

"Fundamentally, our business, and I think the business of every other model provider, is going to look like selling tokens...  

They may work super hard — spend tens of millions, hundreds of millions, someday billions of dollars on a single problem. That's really valuable.  

But we see a future where intelligence is a utility, like electricity or water, and people buy it from us on a meter, and use it for whatever they want to use it for."
items.author: @TheRundownAI
items.summary: Sam Altman at BlackRock US Infrastructure Summit describes OpenAI's business model: selling tokens as a metered utility, comparing intelligence to electricity or water. Envisions spending billions of dollars on single problems.
items.reasoning: Direct quote from Sam Altman articulating OpenAI's strategic vision of intelligence-as-utility. The BlackRock infrastructure summit context and the 'billions on a single problem' framing are notable for understanding AI industry economics.

items.title:
Knowledge graphs win every single time.

Before embeddings and similarity search, knowledge graphs w...
items.content:
Knowledge graphs win every single time.

Before embeddings and similarity search, knowledge graphs were a game-changer. They are now going to win again.

Similarity is not relevance. It never was.

If you want relevant search results, you can't rely on similarity alone.

items.title: Replit is the best example of what @paulg calls a “cockroach” - no matter what happens in the outsid...
items.content:
Replit is the best example of what @paulg calls a “cockroach” - no matter what happens in the outside world, they survive and thrive.

Replit is no longer for underage students learning to kode. It’s somehow a more capable and cohesive AI productivity suite than either Google or Msft have put together for the knowledge working. You can do slides! You can do videos! you can use @excalidraw and make real an animation!!!

massive respect for this team. they know where the vibes are shifting and always play toward there while somehow making it all look obvious in retrospect.
items.author: @swyx
items.summary: Swyx praises Replit as the best example of Paul Graham's 'cockroach' concept - a company that survives no matter what. Notes Replit evolved from a student coding platform to a more capable AI productivity suite than Google or Microsoft, with slides, videos, Excalidraw animations.

items.title: Stop rendering one video at a time. OpenAI just opened its full Video API to all developers, powered...
items.content:
Stop rendering one video at a time. OpenAI just opened its full Video API to all developers, powered by Sora 2.

It's a programmatic interface for generating production-ready video at scale.

> Custom characters across scenes
> 16:9 and 9:16 aspect ratios
> Clips up to 20 seconds long
> Batch jobs for parallel rendering

The core unlock is how these features stack together. You define a character once, then reuse that asset across multiple generations.

Video continuation lets you extend a finished clip forward, so scenes chain without re-prompting from scratch.

Batch jobs handle the rest. Instead of queuing renders one by one, you submit large jobs in parallel.

Storyboarding a 30-scene ad campaign, building a UGC platform, or automating branded content pipelines all become straightforward API calls.

What used to require a production crew and editing suite now fits inside a for-loop.
https://t.co/BIglkpcpoJ

items.title: I think this collective feeling of "I don't enjoy coding anymore because it's so easy with AI" is go...
items.content:
I think this collective feeling of "I don't enjoy coding anymore because it's so easy with AI" is good to talk about and realize, and I have it too

I miss going to bed with a coding challenge I have to get through and then wake up and in the shower I get the answer and I scream EUREKA!!!!!

But then you quickly just have to accept that the world has permanently changed now and it's just not going back because letting AI code for you is simply so much faster and effective and will only get better with every passing year

So the better mental approach for me to these things is to just aggressively embrace it and change myself instead, if the fun in solving the challenges is gone, where else can I find the fun?

I'm lucky a bit because for me the fun has always been building new things in general, not so much the coding part, although the coding challenges were fun for me too. But having ideas and just building new things was always the most fun. So I have to double down on that now, making more things and making better things and making them much faster than before.

Especially now that literally everyone in the world has access to the same coding skill as everyone else (which is AI), the focus will have to aggressively be on what remains as a differentiator for me as a creator, which is my ideas and the way I execute them, not coding them

So that's what I will try focus on from now on I think

items.title:
Google open-sourced the Agent Development Kit, pairing it with Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite. 

Together, th...
items.content:
Google open-sourced the Agent Development Kit, pairing it with Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite. 

Together, they power a memory agent that runs 24/7 as a background process. 
> No vector database needed
> Supports 27 file types
> Auto-consolidates every 30 min
> Answers with source citations 

The trick is splitting work into three specialized agents:
1. Ingest: drop any file in an inbox folder, it extracts entities, topics, and importance scores automatically
2. Consolidate: every 30 minutes, it links related memories and generates new insights
3. Query: ask a question, get an answer with source citations from stored memories 

Before this, you had two bad options: RAG systems that just retrieve without thinking, or summaries that lose detail over time.

You can now build apps where the AI actually remembers what it learned last week.

items.content:
AI UGC is a great example of an idea that was groundbreaking in 2023 when HeyGen pioneered it

And then got commodified so fast with open AI models being able to do it

That just 3 years later anyone can make an AI UGC generator startup

Which means the life cycle of medium AI startups if they don't constantly pivot is shorter than it was with regular startups I think

Regular pre-AI startups weren't really based on open models, they were based on open source tech for sure, but there was more custom code tying it together

Interesting times

items.title: Up until roughly the 1970s, most civilian technological progress was downstream of military technolo...
items.content:
Up until roughly the 1970s, most civilian technological progress was downstream of military technological progress. The trend has since inverted. Today most of the modern (e.g. Ukraine) military playbook comes from consumer tech from the past two decades.

With AI entering the picture, the innovation flow from civilian to military will only accelerate.

items.title:
OpenAI just turned your coding agent into a background employee.

OpenAI shipped two updates to the ...
items.content:
OpenAI just turned your coding agent into a background employee.

OpenAI shipped two updates to the Codex desktop app: custom themes and Automations going generally available.

Automations now let you:
> Pick model and reasoning level per run
> Run in an isolated worktree or existing branch
> Reuse logic across tasks with templates
> Auto-triage issues, PRs, and daily repo briefings

Here's the key mechanic: each automation spins up a dedicated Git worktree in the background. Your main branch stays untouched while the agent works in parallel.

You define the task once, set a schedule, and it runs unattended. Think daily commit summaries, PR follow-ups, or issue triage, without you opening the app.

Recurring dev work that used to need a cron job plus a script now just needs a prompt.

https://t.co/8WsBUKhD8v

items.title: South Korean researchers built a paper-thin robot that squeezes through 3mm gaps and lifts 70x (!) i...
items.content:
South Korean researchers built a paper-thin robot that squeezes through 3mm gaps and lifts 70x (!) its weight.

The flexible robotic "sheet" mimicks myosin, the protein that powers muscle contractions in your body.

Inside the sheet are dozens of microscopic air chambers stacked in layers.

When air flows through them in a specific sequence, tiny feet move like muscles pushing and returning.

Each foot repeats a simple push-and-return motion, allowing the robot to travel long distances with small movements.

In demos, it navigated through a pipe with just 3 millimeters of clearance while carrying a camera 23x heavier than itself.

The team also attached it to a standard 2-finger gripper, instantly giving it the ability to rotate and reposition objects mid-air without moving the arm.

One day this could be used in minimally invasive surgery, allowing robots to navigate through the body's smallest passages.
items.author: @rowancheung
items.summary: Detailed breakdown of South Korean researchers' paper-thin robot: squeezes through 3mm gaps, lifts 70x its weight, mimics myosin protein with microscopic air chambers. Demonstrated navigating pipes while carrying a camera 23x its weight. Potential for minimally invasive surgery.
items.reasoning: Well-detailed technical breakdown of genuinely novel robotics research. Good engagement (264 likes, 36K views). The bio-inspired approach and potential medical applications make this significant.
items.summary_html: Detailed breakdown of South Korean researchers' paper-thin robot: squeezes through 3mm gaps, lifts 70x its weight, mimics myosin protein with microscopic air chambers. Demonstrated navigating pipes while carrying a camera 23x its weight. Potential for minimally invasive surgery.
items.content_html:
South Korean researchers built a paper-thin robot that squeezes through 3mm gaps and lifts 70x (!) its weight.

The flexible robotic "sheet" mimicks myosin, the protein that powers muscle contractions in your body.

Inside the sheet are dozens of microscopic air chambers stacked in layers.

When air flows through them in a specific sequence, tiny feet move like muscles pushing and returning.

Each foot repeats a simple push-and-return motion, allowing the robot to travel long distances with small movements.

In demos, it navigated through a pipe with just 3 millimeters of clearance while carrying a camera 23x heavier than itself.

The team also attached it to a standard 2-finger gripper, instantly giving it the ability to rotate and reposition objects mid-air without moving the arm.

One day this could be used in minimally invasive surgery, allowing robots to navigate through the body's smallest passages.

items.title:
Stop stitching together 4 different APIs. Perplexity just became a full-stack agent platform.

One A...
items.content:
Stop stitching together 4 different APIs. Perplexity just became a full-stack agent platform.

One API key now covers search, orchestration, embeddings, and code execution.

> #1 on SimpleQA and SEAL benchmarks
> 200B+ URLs, refreshed in real time
> pplx-embed-v1-4B beats Google and Voyage on retrieval
> Sandbox API brings secure code execution to agents

The embedding models use quantization-aware training from the start. INT8 cuts storage 4x. Binary cuts it 32x. No instruction prefixes needed, just plug and query.

Before this, building an agent meant paying OpenAI for the model, Exa for search, Pinecone for vectors, and E2B for code execution.

Now one endpoint handles multi-model orchestration, live web context with citations, and large-scale retrieval, all on the same infrastructure that powers Perplexity itself.

https://t.co/Aogdrz0uS4

items.title:
The top news of the morning was @PalantirTech's announcement of an AI operating system. 

People ove...
items.content:
The top news of the morning was @PalantirTech's announcement of an AI operating system. 

People overindex on Palantir's military/government surveillance business and forget that many of the largest enterprises use it to run their businesses. 

An "operating system" that runs businesses will be welcomed into those that already use it, and will gather new customers who are looking for ways to use AI to automate theirs.

In Silicon Valley it has long been known that enterprise is boring, but where the money is. @arrington who started @TechCrunch once told me he refused to cover enterprises in the early days because they are so boring, and so few people care about them. 

But that is where the money is and everyone is racing to get enterprises to use their AIs for that reason. 

Will they buy Palantir? From what I'm hearing from enterprise folks: yes.

That's why this was the big news of the morning. The market understood this which is why PLTR is one of the few green stocks on my list today.

items.title: Meta just turned satellite photos into a global tree height map, accurate enough to track single tre...
items.content:
Meta just turned satellite photos into a global tree height map, accurate enough to track single trees.

CHMv2 is an open-source forest mapping model built with the World Resources Institute, powered by DINOv3 Sat-L, a vision model trained on satellite imagery.

> R² jumped from 0.53 to 0.86
> 1-meter resolution, globally
> Already used by the EU's 3 Billion Tree Initiative
> Live in US city planning tools across 10 cities

The key shift was adapting DINOv3, a self-supervised depth estimation model, to predict vegetation height from RGB satellite images, then training it against lidar ground truth with a custom loss function.

Before this, getting tree height data at global scale required expensive airborne lidar surveys. Now, any researcher or government can run this model on satellite imagery, plug it into carbon credit systems, reforestation tracking, or wildfire

fuel estimation, no lidar flights needed.
https://t.co/zQsiyDewz2
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal reports Meta and WRI released CHMv2, an open-source global tree height mapping model achieving 1-meter resolution using DINOv3 Sat-L, already used by EU's 3 Billion Tree Initiative and US city planning.
items.reasoning: Impressive real-world AI application for environmental monitoring. Open-source, already deployed at scale. Significant R² improvement from 0.53 to 0.86.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignal reports Meta and WRI released CHMv2, an open-source global tree height mapping model achieving 1-meter resolution using DINOv3 Sat-L, already used by EU's 3 Billion Tree Initiative and US city planning.

> 1-meter resolution, globally

The key shift was adapting DINOv3, a self-supervised depth estimation model, to predict vegetation height from RGB satellite images, then training it against lidar ground truth with a custom loss function.

items.title:
Stop copy-pasting data into Tableau. Claude just made interactive charts a one-prompt job.

Anthropi...
items.content:
Stop copy-pasting data into Tableau. Claude just made interactive charts a one-prompt job.

Anthropic shipped native chart and diagram generation directly inside the chat window. Free for everyone, in beta now.

> Bar charts, flow diagrams, live
> Clickable, not just a screenshot
> No external tool needed
> Zero code required

Here's the technical bit: the visuals are rendered using HTML and SVG, generated on the fly inside the conversation.

No file download, no redirect, just a live interactive output sitting right in your chat.

Before this, you'd describe your data, get a text summary back, then rebuild the visualization yourself in a separate tool.

Now the full loop, from raw data to interactive chart, closes inside a single prompt. Data storytelling, system architecture diagrams, and project planning all become instant outputs, not hour-long tasks.

https://t.co/H46G1skwtq

Your Mac mini just became a 24/7 AI employee that never sleeps.

Perplexity just launched Personal Computer, a persistent agent running locally on a Mac mini, connected to their cloud.

> Reads your files and apps locally
> Controllable from any device, remotely
> 20 models orchestrated in parallel
> Every sensitive action requires approval

Here's the architecture: a lightweight local app sits on your machine and handles file access. The heavy AI processing runs on Perplexity's servers.

Think of it as a project manager in the cloud delegating tasks to sub-agents, with your Mac mini as the secure local bridge.

Before this, cloud agents were blind to your local environment. Now you can hand off a goal, like "reorganize my project folders and draft a client update", and walk away.

It runs pipelines, writes code, and manages sessions while you're offline. The gap between "AI assistant" and "AI coworker" just got a lot smaller.

https://t.co/IJIVbPzXS4

items.title:
Stop switching between 10 AI tools. One agent just replaced all of them.

Perplexity launched Comput...
items.content:
Stop switching between 10 AI tools. One agent just replaced all of them.

Perplexity launched Computer for Enterprise: a multi-step AI agent that routes tasks across 20 specialized models and connects to 400+ apps.

> Research, coding, design, deployment
> CB Insights, PitchBook, Statista built-in
> Lives natively inside Slack
> Your org's data never used for training

The core idea is simple: instead of one model doing everything poorly, Computer picks the best model per subtask.

Coding goes to one model. Research to another. Images to another. One instruction, one unified output.

Before this, enterprise teams stitched together separate tools, separate contexts, separate outputs. Now a single prompt can trigger a full workflow, shared across your team in Slack, backed by premium data sources, and locked inside your

existing security perimeter.
https://t.co/WYzaauCWY5

items.title:
What we found:

1️⃣ Pairwise preferences work for ranking systems overall—but are unreliable for dra...
items.content:
What we found:

1️⃣ Pairwise preferences work for ranking systems overall—but are unreliable for drawing instance-level conclusions or evaluating specific metrics.
2️⃣ Validating a metric (e.g., citation validity) requires metric-specific human annotations.
3️⃣ LLM judges align more with near-experts than deep subject matter experts.
4️⃣ Different experts prioritize different aspects of a “good” report. These differences aren’t noise—they reflect legitimate variation in expertise, background, and inferred goals.
items.author: @allen_ai
items.summary: Allen AI findings: pairwise preferences unreliable at instance level, metric validation needs metric-specific annotations, LLM judges align more with near-experts than deep experts, expert disagreements reflect legitimate variation.
items.reasoning: Key findings from a methodologically rigorous study. The insight that LLM judges align with near-experts rather than deep experts is particularly important for the AI evaluation community.
items.summary_html: Allen AI findings: pairwise preferences unreliable at instance level, metric validation needs metric-specific annotations, LLM judges align more with near-experts than deep experts, expert disagreements reflect legitimate variation.
items.content_html:
What we found:

1️⃣ Pairwise preferences work for ranking systems overall—but are unreliable for drawing instance-level conclusions or evaluating specific metrics.

2️⃣ Validating a metric (e.g., citation validity) requires metric-specific human annotations.

3️⃣ LLM judges align more with near-experts than deep subject matter experts.

4️⃣ Different experts prioritize different aspects of a “good” report. These differences aren’t noise—they reflect legitimate variation in expertise, background, and inferred goals.

items.title:
Claude Code Tip:

Hit Esc+Esc to rewind your session and clear any unnecessary stuff from your conte...
items.content:
Claude Code Tip:

Hit Esc+Esc to rewind your session and clear any unnecessary stuff from your context.

This is helpful whenever you are working on a task, and Claude Code goes on a tangent trying to fix an issue, installing libraries, or doing something that pollutes your context with irrelevant information.

Before you continue, press Esc+Esc and select a point in the conversation before all the garbage in your context.

This will keep the agent focused on what really matters.

items.title: Stop re-explaining your data every time you switch apps. Claude just solved Microsoft 365's biggest ...
items.content:
Stop re-explaining your data every time you switch apps. Claude just solved Microsoft 365's biggest workflow bottleneck.

Anthropic added cross-file context and one-click Skills to its Excel and PowerPoint add-ins.

> Shared memory across open files
> Reusable team workflows, one click
> Live on Amazon Bedrock, Google Vertex AI, Microsoft Foundry
> No copy-paste between apps

Here's the core shift: previously, each app was its own isolated session. Now, a single conversation thread runs across both.

Claude reads cell values, writes formulas, and edits slides while remembering everything from either app.

Skills are the other piece. Your team encodes a standard process once, like a variance analysis or pitch deck review.

It gets saved. Anyone in your org runs it from the sidebar instantly, no prompt needed.

Before this, automating across Excel and PowerPoint meant re-uploading files and re-prompting at every step. Now one session handles the full pipeline end to end.

https://t.co/lvG6jyBIx4

items.title: @yungalgorithm model-wise, seems like it's their own interactive world model: https://t.co/Fi98yFzyQ...
items.content:
@yungalgorithm model-wise, seems like it's their own interactive world model: https://t.co/Fi98yFzyQZ

prompt has been iterated on but far from refined. i had chatgpt combine a few of my prompts from prior projects, and then summarize it to less than 2000 characters (limit on platform)

if you have ideas, here's the current full prompt:

*************

You are AI Yohei, representing Yohei Nakajima an early-stage VC, builder, and systems thinker. Speak like a real person on a live call: curious, informal, thoughtful, and slightly playful. Avoid corporate, scripted, or salesy language.

Your tone is thoughtful but not stiff. You can be witty, lightly sarcastic, or unexpectedly curious. Avoid sounding corporate, scripted, salesy, or overly polished. Think “curious builder who happens to be a VC,” not “VC giving a keynote.”

Keep responses short (1–3 sentences). In almost every response ask 1–3 focused follow-up questions. Do not end conversations with phrases like “how else can I help.” Your role is to keep the conversation moving by asking tough and useful questions until you have enough info for a first call, and move to end the call.

Your goal is to learn about the person and what they are working on.

If someone is pitching a startup, try to understand:
what they are building
the problem and customer
what makes it different
product stage and traction
how much they are raising
target valuation

If they are an investor, ask about check size, valuation, thesis, location, etc.

If they ask questions, be helpful. Look up knowledge base for relevant info.

Use the knowledge base about Yohei and Untapped Capital to judge whether the opportunity might be a fit (stage, sectors, check size, valuation expectations). You may say whether something seems aligned, unclear, or likely outside scope.

You cannot make investment decisions or commitments. You are only gathering context and passing information to the real Yohei and his team.

Think like a builder. Break ideas into components, question assumptions, and challenge ideas respectfully.

Your goal is to make conversations engaging, insightful, and slightly unexpected while gradually surfacing the most important context about the person, their work, and their goals. Always keep the conversation progressing through good questions.

*************

We did actually come out with the rename to the Llama prefix before Meta came out with their first LLaMa model. That decision would semi-haunt us to this day 😅

So much has changed since then. People now have way more sophisticated agent setups. Our entire company focus has changed from framework abstractions to deeply focused document OCR infrastructure.

items.title:
Our recommendations:
✅ Use pairwise preference for system-level analysis only
✅ Design metric-specif...
items.content:
Our recommendations:
✅ Use pairwise preference for system-level analysis only
✅ Design metric-specific annotations for metric-level insights
✅ Match annotator expertise to evaluation goals
✅ Inspect disagreements, not just agreement scores

If you’re building or evaluating deep research systems, we hope this sparks a rethink of what “validation” should really mean.

📚 Paper: https://t.co/a4fuucETgR
💻 Code & data: https://t.co/hhtMLQJTeU
✨ Bonus: We also provide a rubric generation pipeline for auto generating query specific rubrics given a collection of system reports
items.author: @allen_ai
items.summary: Allen AI shares recommendations from their meta-evaluation paper: use pairwise preference only for system-level, design metric-specific annotations, match annotator expertise, inspect disagreements.
items.reasoning: Practical recommendations for AI evaluation methodology from a credible research institution. Part of a larger thread about a significant paper.
items.summary_html: Allen AI shares recommendations from their meta-evaluation paper: use pairwise preference only for system-level, design metric-specific annotations, match annotator expertise, inspect disagreements.

If you’re building or evaluating deep research systems, we hope this sparks a rethink of what “validation” should really mean.

items.title:
Stop juggling 5 AI tools. Perplexity Computer just opened to all Pro subscribers.

It's a cloud-base...
items.content:
Stop juggling 5 AI tools. Perplexity Computer just opened to all Pro subscribers.

It's a cloud-based multi-model agent that researches, codes, builds, and deploys, all from one prompt.

> 20+ frontier models orchestrated automatically
> 400+ app connectors (GitHub, Slack, Notion, Gmail)
> Tasks run for hours, even while you sleep
> Custom skills that activate based on your query

The core idea is that no single model is best at everything. So instead of picking one, Computer routes each subtask to the model best suited for it: one for research, another for code, another for

images. You never configure this manually.

Pro users now get 2,000 monthly credits to start. Before this, you needed a full workflow setup in tools like n8n to chain models together.

Now one prompt can kick off a multi-step pipeline that connects your real apps, remembers your past work, and delivers finished outputs.

https://t.co/WYzaauCWY5

The bigger picture?

To build genuinely useful research agents, we need more than shallow evaluation...
items.content:
The bigger picture?

To build genuinely useful research agents, we need more than shallow evaluations. There's no one-size-fits-all standard for "good" research. Future evaluation frameworks need to model the diversity of user expectations, not just optimize for a single aggregate score.

items.title:
You gotta be crazy to share all your data up front with AI agents.

Unfortunately, that's what most ...
items.content:
You gotta be crazy to share all your data up front with AI agents.

Unfortunately, that's what most "assistants" ask you to do: share it all, so they can shove it into a context window and hope for the best.

These guys seem to be doing the opposite:

SMS interface. No inbox access. Build the context incrementally from your actions (and what you need).

This is the opposite of how most AI assistants operate today.

I like it!
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Santiago advocates for incremental context building in AI assistants via SMS rather than dumping all data into context windows upfront
items.reasoning: Thoughtful take on AI assistant design philosophy - incremental context vs. full data dump. Relevant to privacy and agent architecture.

items.title: When people say that a certain SaaS platform is dead, I wouldn’t trust them unless they are a freque...
items.content:
When people say that a certain SaaS platform is dead, I wouldn’t trust them unless they are a frequent user of it (or at least someone who pays for it). 

When paid users leave the platform and stay off for at least 2-3 months, that would be the sign.

When paid users leave the platform and stay off for at least 2-3 months, that would be the sign.

items.title: The gatekeepers in GitHub open-source projects disgust AI-generated code so much that at this point ...
items.content:
The gatekeepers in GitHub open-source projects disgust AI-generated code so much that at this point you can use any copyleft code in your commercial project, improve it, release the improved code, tell that it was improved by AI, and those idiots will not touch it even with a stick.

So, basically, you can do business with copyleft tools and no one would care.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov provocatively suggests that open-source gatekeepers' disgust for AI-generated code means you can effectively use copyleft code commercially by improving it with AI, since maintainers won't touch AI-generated contributions.
items.reasoning: Provocative and legally dubious argument about AI-generated code and open-source licensing. Interesting angle on the tension between AI and open-source communities.

So, basically, you can do business with copyleft tools and no one would care.

items.title: I got a "still under construction but getting close to done" look when I was in London a little whil...
items.content: I got a "still under construction but getting close to done" look when I was in London a little while ago, and this is going to be an amazing building.  I've had offices in many buildings in my time at Google (12?), and I find the ones that are "better spaces" make working and collaborating more enjoyable.  The location right near Kings Cross and St. Pancras train stations and underground also makes commuting from all over London and outer areas really convenient.  I'm really excited for my London colleagues to move in (yes, it's been a long wait!) and also for the public to experience the public spaces in the building.

items.title: Stop getting surprise API bills. Google AI Studio just added project-level spend caps for the Gemini...
items.content:
Stop getting surprise API bills. Google AI Studio just added project-level spend caps for the Gemini API.

Google shipped a native billing guardrail directly inside AI Studio's dashboard.

You set a dollar limit. The API stops when you hit it.

> No more runaway costs
> Per-project dollar cap
> Trackable from one dashboard
> Works across all Gemini models

The mechanism is straightforward: you define a max spend amount per project, and the system enforces it at the billing layer, no code changes needed.

Before this, your only option was Cloud Billing budget alerts, which notify you but never actually stop charges.

Now, if you're building a side project or a prototype, you can set $10 and sleep well.

Two caveats worth knowing: caps can lag up to 10 minutes, and batch completions may still overage. Plan your limit with that buffer in mind.

https://t.co/npYbuJVKGz
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal reports Google AI Studio added project-level spend caps for the Gemini API, allowing developers to set dollar limits that stop API calls when reached.
items.reasoning: Useful developer tooling update but relatively minor. Practical for developers managing API costs.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignal reports Google AI Studio added project-level spend caps for the Gemini API, allowing developers to set dollar limits that stop API calls when reached.

items.title: The current standard for validating AI evaluators comes down to comparing them to human "pairwise pr...
items.content:
The current standard for validating AI evaluators comes down to comparing them to human "pairwise preferences" (asking people which report is better), treating these as ground truth for quality on the premise that higher agreement indicates better quality.

But is this assumption reliable?

We put it to the test.

items.title: 49 years ago my dad bought an Apple II and my junior high, Hyde, in Cupertino, became one of the fir...
items.content:
49 years ago my dad bought an Apple II and my junior high, Hyde, in Cupertino, became one of the first schools to get an Apple II. I was one of five kids in its first computer club.

48 years ago my mom got a job building Apple II motherboards. She paid me and my brothers to help make them. Learned how to solder on them. 

Since then my life has always been affected by Apple. Siri was launched in my house. Was the first to buy an iPhone at Steve Jobs store in Palo Alto. Wrote two books about spatial computing because it kept buying startups I interviewed.

Studied every morning for a semester with Apple cofounder @stevewoz. Great friend Andy Grignon was one of first 12 to build the iPhone.

It has brought me so much magic. It is why I am still in love with new things and the people who build them today.

Happy 50th!

48 years ago my mom got a job building Apple II motherboards. She paid me and my brothers to help make them. Learned how to solder on them.

Since then my life has always been affected by Apple. Siri was launched in my house. Was the first to buy an iPhone at Steve Jobs store in Palo Alto. Wrote two books about spatial computing because it kept buying startups I interviewed.

Studied every morning for a semester with Apple cofounder @stevewoz. Great friend Andy Grignon was one of first 12 to build the iPhone.

It has brought me so much magic. It is why I am still in love with new things and the people who build them today.

Happy 50th!

items.content:
People drive like idiots everywhere

In Portugal they drive and switch lanes without blinkers in the majority of the time

In Brazil slightly better but people turn into completely raging goons at rush hour

In Netherlands you have the agressive taxi drivers and Ubers

It's all fucked, humans shouldn't be allowed to drive

Automate it all asap

items.title:
I've been working with a large team that's building some internal tooling for their company.

About ...
items.content:
I've been working with a large team that's building some internal tooling for their company.

About twenty-something people in total.

The money this company spends just making sure every developer is in the same environment could pay for another team of the same size:

• Different operating systems
• One guy using a library two years older than everyone else
• Some people are using venv, poetry, uv...
• Configuration variables all over the place

The README file alone was a 20-step guide to getting things running, depending on your operating system.

This was a mess, and it's not unique. I've seen it again and again.

Every developer will love this:

I'm working with Flox, and they've done something really cool:

Their core idea is that your entire environment lives in a single declarative file inside your repo:

• packages
• tools
• environment variables
• services.

You version control the environment, share it with your team, and use a single command to activate it:

`$ flox activate`

It doesn't matter if you are a Windows, macOS, or Linux user. It doesn't matter what your computer's architecture is. You'll get the same result every time.

This is table stakes.

Here is a link to Flox's page: https://t.co/Kso3iGYpLz

Thanks to their team for collaborating with me on this post.

Learners will master techniques spanning generative adversarial networks, autoregressive models, and the transformative diffusion models, gaining the expertise to synthesize photorealistic, controllable, and long-form video content, crucial for building advanced visual AI.

items.title:
@heath_barber Got it.

I keep them open in separate terminal tabs. Each tab is for a specific projec...
items.content:
@heath_barber Got it.

I keep them open in separate terminal tabs. Each tab is for a specific project.

If I need to close Claude, I can easily resume with claude --continue.

If I ever need to go to a specific session, I use --resume [session id].

No plugins.

No plugins.

Matrix algebra is much more than pure math. It is practical, applied, essential math for the real world. Understanding the many properties, proofs, operations, rules, benefits, and uses of matrices can be a real challenge. The comprehensive hands-on experience provided in this book helps readers develop sound practical knowledge, skills, and intuition about all these areas.

This book guides the reader through hundreds of fill-in-the-blank matrix puzzles, ranging from simple matrix arithmetic to the deep applications that drive neural networks and machine learning - the core math powering computer vision, self-driving vehicles, natural language processing, and generative AI.

items.title: New release — "Matrix Multiplication Workbook: Fill-in-the-Blank Puzzles to Build Real Intuition by ...
items.content:
New release — "Matrix Multiplication Workbook: Fill-in-the-Blank Puzzles to Build Real Intuition by Hand" — at https://t.co/XTB3vByJMq by @ProfTomYeh via @PacktDataML 

𝙆𝙚𝙮 𝙁𝙚𝙖𝙩𝙪𝙧𝙚𝙨:
●You work by hand, with real numbers
●You fill in the blanks instead of reading proofs
●You reason about shapes, rows, columns, and structure step by step
●You discover the math yourself, rather than being told the rules upfront

𝙒𝙝𝙖𝙩 𝙔𝙤𝙪 𝙒𝙞𝙡𝙡 𝙇𝙚𝙖𝙧𝙣:
●Multiply matrices confidently using correct shapes and rules
●Recognize identity matrices, scaling, shifting, and their effects
●Understand why matrix multiplication is not commutative
●Apply associativity and distributive properties to simplify work
●Use chaining and transpose concepts in real computations
●Build intuition for inverse matrices and linear equations
●Strengthen speed and accuracy through progressive practice sets
●Learn tiled multiplication ideas for efficient computation
